Document Title: Protection Of Sensitive Personal Information Contained In Digital Files Held By Individuals With Homomorphic Encryption And AI Assistance
Authors: E. A. Villegas-Jiménez; A. Romo-Cabrera; M. A. Manzano-Calixto; L. M. Tello-Aguilera; A. K. Sánchez-Mata; A. Robles-Guerrero; P. M. Hernández-Ruiz
Author Affiliations: Facultad de Estudios Superiores "Aragón", Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Estado de México, México; Facultad de Estudios Superiores "Aragón", Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Estado de México, México; Facultad de Estudios Superiores "Aragón", Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Estado de México, México; Facultad de Estudios Superiores "Aragón", Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Estado de México, México; Facultad de Estudios Superiores "Aragón", Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Estado de México, México; Facultad de Estudios Superiores "Aragón", Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Estado de México, México; Facultad de Estudios Superiores "Aragón", Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Estado de México, México
Publication Title: 2025 5th International Conference on Electrical, Computer and Energy Technologies (ICECET)
Abstract: In today’s digital landscape, the exchange of sensitive personal data via mobile devices is widespread, yet inadequate safeguards often expose users to risks such as identity theft and online harassment. This research introduces Kamplae, a system crafted in Python and deployed through a Flask-based REST API, employing homomorphic encryption and artificial intelligence to protect personal information embedded in digital images. Kamplae secures metadata and bitmap content in jpeg, jpg, and png formats, adhering to legal standards in Mexico and the European Union.The study reviews prior work on personal data protection and the challenges posed by deep fakes, emphasising the need for robust regulatory frameworks to counter digital violence. It explores various encryption methods—symmetric, asymmetric, and hybrid—alongside relevant legal provisions across regions.Kamplae aims to prevent image misuse and foster a secure, ethical digital environment. While promising, it highlights areas for enhancement, such as broader format support and user interface refinement, with limitations tied to specific format compatibility and mobile performance.
Author Keywords: deep fake;encryption;hormomorphic encryption;artificial intelligence;symmetric encryption;asymmetric encryption;multimedia files
IEEE Terms: Filtering;Filters;Deepfakes;Protocols;Videos;Cyberspace;Mobile handsets;Social computing;Cyberbullying;Communication systems

Publication Title: 2025 5th International Conference on Electrical, Computer and Energy Technologies (ICECET)
Abstract: The increasing popularity of the use of AI in everyday life has enabled the development of virtual systems for human assistance. This research proposes an implemented linear regression model with a vector of random weights, using a mean square error (MSE), based cost function and regularisation and corresponding Python code that integrates AI-based haptic feedback through vibration, specifically designed to optimise weight training.The results of the simulation model analysis show how key biomechanical parameters are identified and used to improve a person's posture by adjusting technique through real-time vibrational stimuli. The development of the system involves physical benefits such as injury prevention, continuous biomechanical monitoring, adaptive programme structuring and haptic vibration feedback for immediate movement correction. Popular and accurate tools for building linguistic models are mentioned, such as machine learning, computer vision and neural networks. Technological tools such as machine learning, computer vision and neural networks, applied to process biomechanical data and trigger personalised vibrational responses, are highlighted.

Document Title: Pseudo-Temporal Modeling with 2D CNNs for Framewise Surgical Gesture Segmentation in Robot-Assisted Surgery
Authors: D. Haro-Mendoza; G. A. Castro Vazquez; A. Leonardo Campos Carreras; A. J. Diaz Urias; V. J. Gonzalez-Villela; M. Cedillo-Hernandez; R. E. Arevalo-Ancona
Author Affiliations: Dept. of Mechatronics Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Dept. of Mechatronics Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Dept. of Biomedical Systems Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Dept. of Mechatronics Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Dept. of Mechatronics Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; SEPI ESIME Culhuacán, Instituto Politécnico Nacional, Mexico City, Mexico; SEPI ESIME Culhuacán, Instituto Politécnico Nacional, Mexico City, Mexico
Publication Title: 2025 6th International Conference on Communications, Information, Electronic and Energy Systems (CIEES)
Abstract: Surgical procedures can be decomposed into elementary units (tasks, surgemes, and dexemes) that represent the building blocks of surgical performance. Segmenting gestures at this level is crucial for objective skill assessment and for understanding how simple movements are concatenated to form complete procedures, ultimately supporting the development of training and evaluation systems. In this work, we present a pseudo-temporal modeling approach for surgical gesture segmentation using a 2D ResNet-50 convolutional network trained exclusively on RGB frames. During training, each video was divided into eight equal segments, which were used to feed the network and generate gesture-level probabilities. To optimize learning, an adaptive Exponential Moving Average (EMA) mechanism was applied to stabilize weight updates and reinforce poorly performing gesture classes. At inference, complete surgical videos were processed with an overlapping sliding window of eight frames, where each frame could be evaluated multiple times in different contexts. Frame-level probabilities were averaged across overlapping windows and refined through a temporal mode filter, producing stable and coherent gesture sequences. The method was validated on the JIGSAWS dataset, which includes the Suturing, Knot Tying, and Needle Passing tasks. Results showed reliable recognition across all procedures: Suturing achieved 82.26%, Knot Tying 91.97%, and Needle Passing 79.35%. Training required approximately one hour, and inference on a complete video of 2950 frames was performed in about 45 seconds, also generating a labeled output video. By relying solely on 2D convolutions and RGB input, the approach achieves accurate and efficient segmentation with low computational cost, making it suitable for surgical training environments with limited resources and applicable in near real-time scenarios.
Author Keywords: surgical gesture segmentation;pseudo-temporal modeling;2d convolutional neural networks;resnet 50;exponential moving average
IEEE Terms: Training;Adaptation models;Accuracy;Computational modeling;Surgery;Needles;Real-time systems;Windows;Convolutional neural networks;Videos

Publication Title: 2025 21st International Symposium on Biomedical Image Processing and Analysis (SIPAIM)
Abstract: Fetal femur length measurement via ultrasound, alongside abdominal circumference, biparietal diameter, and cranial circumference assessments, are key components of prenatal screening, as they enable the estimation of fetal weight and the evaluation of intrauterine development. Currently, this procedure still heavily relies on manual intervention by expert radiologists or sonographers, who must identify anatomical landmarks to perform the measurements. This dependence on specialized human expertise limits diagnostic accessibility in remote areas far from major urban centers. In this work, we propose a novel convolutional neural network trained on binary masks and heatmaps of femur endpoints. The model achieves robust performance, segmenting the femur with an 88% Dice coefficient, and measuring its length with only a 2.515% error margin. This work is part of a broader machine learning based system for fetal weight estimation, aiming to expand diagnostic capabilities to healthcare centers with limited human resources.

Authors: G. Carcedo-Rodríguez; B. Vazquez; J. Perez-Gonzalez; N. Hevia-Montiel
Author Affiliations: Master Program in Computer Science and Engineering, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mérida, Yucatán, México; Instituto de Investigaciones en Matemáticas Aplicadas y en Sistemas, Unidad Académica en el Estado de Yucatán, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mérida, Yucatán, México; Instituto de Investigaciones en Matemáticas Aplicadas y en Sistemas, Unidad Académica en el Estado de Yucatán, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mérida, Yucatán, México; Instituto de Investigaciones en Matemáticas Aplicadas y en Sistemas, Unidad Académica en el Estado de Yucatán, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mérida, Yucatán, México
Publication Title: 2025 21st International Symposium on Biomedical Image Processing and Analysis (SIPAIM)
Abstract: Ultrasound is widely used in medical imaging due to its accessibility and cost-effectiveness. This paper presents a Deep Learning-based approach for enhancing echocardiographic imaging by addressing two challenges: speckle noise reduction and temporal resolution improvement. For denoising task, we propose the SE-U-Net model, while for temporal enhancement we employ Real-Time Intermediate Flow Estimation for frame interpolation. Multiple datasets were used for training, validation, and testing of both models. The proposed pipeline was applied in high heart rate murine models to enhance relevant information. In final evaluation, the denoising model achieved SSIM=0.99 and PSNR=38.27dB, indicating effective contrast enhancement, preservation of homogeneous regions, and reduction of small artifacts. The interpolation model, on the other hand, achieved SSIM=0.87 and PSNR=33.23dB, enabling the increase of ultrasound temporal resolution from 37 to 296 frames per second without losing image quality.

Document Title: Smart Machine Vision for Universal Spatial-Mode Reconstruction
Authors: J. D. Huerta-Morales; C. You; O. S. Magaña-Loaiza; S. -H. Dong; R. d. J. León-Montiel; M. A. Quiroz-Juárez
Author Affiliations: Instituto de Ciencias Nucleares, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Ciudad de México, Mexico; Department of Physics and Astronomy, Quantum Photonics Laboratory, Louisiana State University, Baton Rouge, LA, USA; Department of Physics and Astronomy, Quantum Photonics Laboratory, Louisiana State University, Baton Rouge, LA, USA; Research Center for Quantum Physics, Huzhou University, Huzhou, China; Instituto de Ciencias Nucleares, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Ciudad de México, Mexico; Centro de Física Aplicada y Tecnología Avanzada, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Querétaro, Mexico
Publication Title: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
Abstract: Structured light beams, in particular, those carrying orbital angular momentum (OAM), have gained a lot of attention due to their potential for enlarging the transmission capabilities of communication systems. However, the use of OAM-carrying light in communications faces two major problems, namely distortions introduced during propagation in disordered media, such as the atmosphere or optical fibers, and the large divergence that high-order OAM modes experience. While the use of nonorthogonal modes may offer a way to circumvent the divergence of high-order OAM fields, artificial intelligence (AI) algorithms have shown promise for solving the mode-distortion issue. Unfortunately, current AI-based algorithms make use of large-amount data-handling protocols that generally lead to large processing time and high power consumption. Here, we show that a low-power, low-cost image sensor can act as an artificial neural network that simultaneously detects and reconstructs distorted OAM-carrying beams. We demonstrate the capabilities of our device by reconstructing (with a 95% efficiency) individual Vortex, Laguerre-Gaussian (LG), and Bessel modes, as well as hybrid (nonorthogonal) coherent superpositions of such modes. Our work provides a potentially useful basis for the development of low-power-consumption, light-based communication devices.

Document Title: Outlet Pressure Regulation in a High-Viscosity Two-Phase Flow Horizontal Pipeline Using Inverse ANN and PSO
Authors: W. Ajbar; L. Torres; J. E. Guzmán Vázquez; M. Cervantes-Bobadilla
Author Affiliations: Instituto de Ingeniería, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Instituto de Ingeniería, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Instituto de Ingeniería, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Tecnológico Nacional de México/CENIDET, Cuernavaca, Morelos, México
Publication Title: 2024 10th International Conference on Control, Decision and Information Technologies (CoDIT)
Abstract: This article presents the development of two models based on artificial neural networks (ANN) aimed at estimating the pressure at a position near the output of a horizontal pipeline that transports a two-phase flow of glycerin-air, which is characterized by its high viscosity. These models consist of an input layer that includes variables such as the air flow rate (Qa), the glycerin flow rate (Qg), and the pressure at three spatial points located before the desired pressure prediction point. In the hidden layer of the first model, the nonlinear activation function Logsig is employed, while in the second model, the Tansig function is used. In both cases, a linear function is used in the output layer. In order to determine the air and glycerin flow rates that were injected to the pipeline, ANN models ares inverted by using the particle swarm optimization (PSO) algorithm. From these inverted models, the design of an open-loop control to regulate the output pressure in the horizontal pipeline is presented. This control allows simultaneous manipulation of the Qg and Qa variables, or a single Qg variable, enabling precise regulation.
IEEE Terms: Viscosity;Training;Atmospheric modeling;Pipelines;Transfer functions;Transportation;Artificial neural networks;Predictive models;Regulation;Particle swarm optimization

Document Title: Early Predictive Modeling of Engineering Student Attrition: An Ai-Driven Machine Learning Approach
Authors: M. F. E. Posadas; N. Á. Esquivel; J. I. V. Sánchez
Author Affiliations: Division of Physical-Mathematical, Sciences and Engineering, FES Aragón, Universidad Nacional, Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Division of Physical-Mathematical, Sciences and Engineering, FES Aragón, Universidad Nacional, Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Division of Physical-Mathematical, Sciences and Engineering, FES Aragón, Universidad Nacional, Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico
Publication Title: 2025 World Engineering Education Forum - Global Engineering Deans Council (WEEF-GEDC)
Abstract: Academic trajectory tracking is a crucial tool for evaluating student performance and predicting graduation outcomes. In engineering education, early identification of students at risk of not completing their studies can enable timely intervention. This study focuses on developing an Artificial Intelligence (AI) predictive model for engineering students at the Faculty of Higher Studies Aragón (FES Aragón) of the National Autonomous University of Mexico (UNAM), utilizing a classification approach based on early-semester performance indicators. The goal of this study is to develop an algorithm that can identify students who, based on their academic trajectory patterns, may be at risk of dropping out. By identifying these students early, targeted interventions such as tutoring, remedial courses, and other support strategies can be implemented to help them stay on track and complete their university studies. The dataset consists of academic records from multiple student cohorts. Grades from the first and second semesters were encoded to represent exam attempts and passing conditions. Several Artificial Intelligence based classification models were tested using MATLAB. Feature importance was evaluated through correlation matrices and Parallel Coordinates Plot analysis. Adjusting training-test splits and modifying classification costs were also assessed to enhance model performance. Preliminary results indicate that when using first- and second-semester grades alone, classification accuracy reaches 72.48 %, with models such as the Ensemble Subspace K-Nearest Neighbors demonstrating an Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUC) of 0.7827. For prediction incorporating data up to the third semester, models achieved a highest accuracy of 78.85 %, with the Medium Gaussian Support Vector Machines model demonstrating a high AUC of 0.8438. Current work focuses on optimizing the model through feature selection and fine-tuning classification weights to enhance predictive capabilities. This work explores the potential of AI-driven predictive modeling in identifying students at risk of academic attrition. While classification models achieve reasonable accuracy, further refinements are necessary. Future work will investigate the integration of additional contextual factors, such as socioeconomic variables and engagement metrics, to enhance the model's effectiveness.
Author Keywords: machine learning;predictive modeling;student attrition;student retention;higher education
IEEE Terms: Support vector machines;Accuracy;Target tracking;Trajectory tracking;Machine learning;Predictive models;Data models;Trajectory;Engineering students;Socioeconomics

Author Affiliations: Posgrado de Ingeniería Eléctrica, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Facultad de Ingeniería, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Facultad de Ingeniería, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Centro Médico ABC, Mexico city, Mexico
Publication Title: 2025 IEEE 38th International Symposium on Computer-Based Medical Systems (CBMS)
Abstract: Standard view cardiac computed tomography (CT) images are of great importance in clinical practice due to the valuable diagnostic information they provide. However, navigating these (3D) medical images to identify such a view can represent a time-consuming process, subject to inter-operator variability. This study presents an approach using a Multiscale Vision Transformer (MViT) to infer the rotation parameters required to obtain standard views and subsequently focus on extracting the left ventricular outflow tract (LVOT) view. Once this view is obtained, the aortic valve is detected and segmented using well-known convolutional neural networks (CNNs), specifically ResNet18 for detection and U-Net for segmentation. The methodology used for its training is detailed, along with the metrics employed to evaluate their performance. Finally, the results obtained for each stage are presented. This approach provides a clear understanding of the performance of each model, from rotation parameter prediction to aortic valve detection and segmentation.

Document Title: MMDA: A Multimodal and Multisource Domain Adaptation Method for Cross-Subject Emotion Recognition From EEG and Eye Movement Signals
Authors: M. Jiménez-Guarneros; G. Fuentes-Pineda; J. Grande-Barreto
Author Affiliations: Department of Computer Science, Instituto de Investigaciones en Matemáticas Aplicadas y en Sistemas (IIMAS), Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México (UNAM), Coyoacán, Ciudad de México, Mexico; Department of Computer Science, Instituto de Investigaciones en Matemáticas Aplicadas y en Sistemas (IIMAS), Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México (UNAM), Coyoacán, Ciudad de México, Mexico; Information Technology Engineering, Universidad Politécnica de Puebla, Cuanalá, Puebla, Mexico
Publication Title: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems
Abstract: Multimodal emotion recognition from electroencephalogram (EEG) and eye movement signals has shown to be a promising approach to provide more discriminative information about human emotional states. However, most current works rely on a subject-dependent approach, limiting their applicability to new users. Recently, some studies have explored multimodal domain adaptation to address the mentioned issue by transferring information from known subjects to new ones. Unfortunately, existing methods are still exposed to negative transfer as a suboptimal distribution alignment is performed between subjects, while irrelevant information is not discarded. In this article, we present a multimodal and multisource domain adaptation (MMDA) method, which adopts the following three strategies: 1) marginal and conditional distribution alignments must be performed between each known subject and a new one; 2) relevant distribution alignments must be prioritized to avoid a negative transfer; and 3) modality fusion results should be improved by extracting more discriminative features from EEG signals and selecting relevant features across modalities. Our proposed method was evaluated with leave-one-subject-out cross validation on four public datasets: SEED, SEED-GER, SEED-IV, and SEED-V. Experimental results show that our proposal outperforms state-of-the-art results for each dataset when subject data from different sessions are combined into a single dataset. Moreover, MMDA exceeds the state of the art in 8 out of 11 different sessions when each session is evaluated.
Author Keywords: Deep learning;electroencephalogram (EEG);eye movement (EM);multimodal emotion recognition (MER);multimodal unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA);multisource domain adaptation (MDA)
IEEE Terms: Electroencephalography;Emotion recognition;Neural networks;Feature extraction;Data models;Training;Physiology;Correlation;Brain modeling;Adaptation models

Publication Title: 2025 6th International Conference on Control and Fault-Tolerant Systems (SysTol)
Abstract: This paper proposes a method to estimate both the friction factor and the roughness coefficient of the pipes in a water distribution network (WDN). Pressure head data is used for the training of a Long-Short Term Memory (LSTM) neural network to predict the pressure head at all of the other nodes in the network, as well as the flow rate through the pipes. When all the data is predicted, the estimated pressure head drops, and the flow rate for the entire water distribution network is used to compute both the friction factor and the flow rate. The proposed method is tested using the well-known Hanoi DMA (District Metered Area) as a case study. Satisfactory results are obtained when comparing the predicted friction factor and roughness coefficient against the expected values.

Abstract: This paper applies linear attention methods (LAMs) for creating models that recognize voltage sag, swell and harmonics in high voltage alternate current (HVAC) for a monophase transmission line. The method can simultaneously predict all the faults while working with various windows frames even as small as a half cycle. The approach is based on using data and allowing the model to learn the mathematical relationship between the input and the expected output by itself. The usage of LAMs made effective and precise predictions, in addition to the faster processing of information as compared with traditional attention models. The inclusion of different types of noises has not greatly affected the model’s performance, which produces a reliable method for identifying these types of distortions.

Author Affiliations: DEPI, Tecnológico Nacional de México / Instituto Tecnólogico de Morelia, Morelia, México; DEPI, Tecnológico Nacional de México / Instituto Tecnólogico de Morelia, Morelia, México; DEPI, Tecnológico Nacional de México / Instituto Tecnólogico de Morelia, Morelia, México; Instituto de Geofisica Unidad Morelia, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Morelia, México; DEPI, Tecnológico Nacional de México / Instituto Tecnólogico de Morelia, Morelia, México; DEPI, Tecnológico Nacional de México / Instituto Tecnólogico de Morelia, Morelia, México; DEPI, Tecnológico Nacional de México / Instituto Tecnólogico de Morelia, Morelia, México
Publication Title: 2025 IEEE International Autumn Meeting on Power, Electronics and Computing (ROPEC)
Abstract: This paper presents a machine learning approach for the automatic classification of synthetic Jovian radio emissions using spectrogram analysis. As a continuation of previous work involving the design of a dipole antenna and the development of a Python-based acquisition system centered at 20.1 MHz, this study focuses on generating a synthetic dataset that replicates the Io-A, Io-B and Io-C radio emissions of Jupiter. Each class was modeled using Python scripts that incorporated variability in position, intensity, shape and noise within the 16-24Mhz frequency range. Two classification models were trained and evaluated: a convolutional neural network (efficientNet-B0) and a vision transformer (ViT Tiny Patch16-224). Both models were tested using a 70/15/15 train-validation-test split and validated with 5-fold cross-validation. The ViT model achieved up to 100% classification accuracy across all folds, outperforming the CNN in consistency and generalization. This synthetic classification framework serves as a preparatory phase for future work involving the training and validation of these models using real labeled spectrograms extracted from the Juno/Waves database, which includes emission records from 2016 to 2023.

Abstract: Multimodal physiological data from electroencephalogram (EEG) and eye movement (EM) signals have been shown to be useful in effectively recognizing human emotional states. Unfortunately, individual differences reduce the applicability of existing multimodal classifiers to new users, as low performance is usually observed. Indeed, existing works mainly focus on multimodal domain adaptation from a labeled source domain and unlabeled target domain to address the mentioned problem, transferring knowledge from known subjects to new one. However, a limited set of labeled target data has not been effectively exploited to enhance the knowledge transfer between subjects. In this article, we propose a multimodal semi-supervised domain adaptation (SSDA) method, called cross-modal learning and joint distribution alignment (CMJDA), to address the limitations of existing works, following three strategies: 1) discriminative features are exploited per modality through independent neural networks; 2) correlated features and consistent predictions are produced between modalities; and 3) marginal and conditional distributions are encouraged to be similar between the labeled source data, limited labeled target data, and abundant unlabeled target data. We conducted comparison experiments on two public benchmarks for emotion recognition, SEED-IV and SEED-V, using leave-one-out cross-validation (LOOCV). Our proposal achieves an average accuracy of 92.50%–96.13% across the three available sessions on SEED-IV and SEED-V, only including three labeled target samples per class from the first recorded trial.

Abstract: A dual-panel Positron Emission Mammography (PEM) scanner is a dedicated breast imaging device with higher spatial resolution and enhanced sensitivity, in comparison with conventional Positron Emission Tomography (PET) scanners. However, one of the main challenges this scanner faces are the limited angle artifacts that arise in the cross-planes. To mitigate such artifacts, deep learning regularization has been included in the List Mode Ordered Subsets Expectation Maximization (LM-OSEM) reconstruction algorithm. Regularization has been applied using a U-Net trained with Monte-Carlo simulated data. Network training was achieved using slices of cross-plane images of a PEM scanner as input and corresponding slices from a dedicated breast-PET ring scanner as target, since this scanner does not have the limited angle problem. In the regularization stage, the trained U-Net is employed to process the cross-plane slices of the preceding image estimation. Subsequently, the resultant output is fused with the former image estimate utilizing Forward-backward splitting expectation maximization (FBSEM). This image reconstruction framework has demonstrated a notable enhancement in image quality when assessed from the cross-plane perspective, while maintaining the quality of images when assessed from the in-plane perspective. With this approach, PEM images may be effectively analyzed as three-dimensional images.

Abstract: A dual-panel Positron Emission Mammography (PEM) scanner prototype is under development in our research group, and its imaging performance is currently being evaluated. The main challenge for dual-panel scanners is the presence of limited-angle artifacts, which appear in the images due to the acquisition geometry. These artifacts diminish the quality of the images, degrade the contrast of the lesions, and generate distortions in the cross-plane slices (perpendicular to the panels). To mitigate such distortions, deep learning regularization has been incorporated into the List Mode Ordered Subsets Expectation Maximization (LM-OSEM) image reconstruction algorithm. For the regularization step, a U-Net was trained with Monte-Carlo simulated data for several image quality phantoms. Data augmentation strategies were used to increase the diversity of the training data and to construct a model that generalizes more effectively to unseen data. The deep learning model was trained with 3840 pairs of cross-plane images of a dual-panel PEM scanner as input and corresponding slices from a dedicated breast-PET ring scanner as target. This approach was selected because a ring-shaped scanner does not face the limited-angle problem. The Forward-backward splitting expectation maximization (FBSEM) algorithm was implemented with the trained U-Net. This framework was applied to both MC-simulated and experimentally acquired data. In both cases, the quality of the cross-plane images was enhanced, without hampering the in-plane slices (parallel to the panels), and the enhancement in the quality of the images was quantified by image quality metrics like noise, contrast, and the Mean Absolute Error (MAE).

Abstract: A user model (UM) is a representation of the characteristics, behaviors, and preferences of a user that a computer system utilizes to provide the user with personalized experiences. In educational settings, for instance, a system can use information about the Learning Styles (LSs) of a student to provide them with adequate study materials. Furthermore, with the growing use of Learning Management System (LMS) platforms, such as Moodle, continuous streams of user data highlight the need to compute student models dynamically to support adaptive decision-making in teaching and learning. This paper introduces a framework for Dynamic User Modeling (DUMING) to create evolving Dynamic User Models (DUMs) that capture and adapt to changes in student LSs over time, based on event log streams from an LMS. This framework leverages the Felder and Silverman Learning Styles Model (FSLSM) and integrates Data Stream Mining (DSM) and Process Mining (PM) techniques across three layers: collection, preprocessing, and mining. Collected event streams are processed within landmark windows and classified into High-Level Events (HLEs) based on LMS functionality. The STREAMKMeans algorithm clusters events, and the PM phase uses Alpha Miner (AM), Heuristic Miner (HM), and Inductive Miner (IM) algorithms to identify learning paths represented as Petri Nets (PNs), with IM showing the highest values in the quality metrics used. Implemented and evaluated in a course using Moodle at the Universidad Veracruzana in Mexico, the framework employs IF-THEN rules to relate HLE proportions to FSLSM-based LSs, visualizing changes through radar charts. The results demonstrate the effectiveness of the framework in generating adaptive DUMs, providing insights that enhance over time educational support and enable timely interventions.

Abstract: This paper introduces a novel adaptive feedback control approach for disturbed chains of integrators with smooth disturbances with unknown upper bound. These characteristics contribute to its simplicity and lead to improved closed-loop performance. Abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI), with machine learning (ML) models and deep learning (DL) neural networks, represents a possibility for detecting DoS/DDoS attacks more effectively. The most commonly used evaluation metrics are Accuracy, Recall, Precision, and F1-Score, and the most frequently found public datasets include NSL-KDD, CICDoS2019, and CICIDS2017. A wide variety of LSTM-based models and approaches that can be used to experiment and compare their performance in terms of communications network security.

Abstract: The growth in distributed energy resources (DERs) is an important step toward solving the global climate crisis. Such perturbations degrade the performance of data-driven algorithms introduced in power grids for sensing and control. Existing approaches can strengthen machine learning models against well-designed malicious attacks. However, such physics-agnostic efforts fail to ensure the robustness of natural perturbations in power grids, that occur due to varying loads and changes in control inputs. This article proposes a novel physics-constrained adversarial training method for robustifying neural networks for the crucial problem of locating edge-faults in power grids. Our approach relies on first deriving analytical physical laws that are satisfied by state perturbations in realistic grids and then using the descriptive sets during adversarial training. Compared to state-of-the-art methods, our perturbation-robust neural networks have higher robust accuracy as well as training efficiency. Also, we demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves a tighter upper bound of robust risks than traditional efforts. The numerical experimental results in the IEEE 68-bus benchmark system validate our adversarial training in two scenarios when loads and control inputs vary randomly. The proposed method shows superior performance in accuracy and efficiency for different perturbed datasets. In addition, we testify the influence of different inputs on the robustness.

Abstract: Drug discovery is an intricate and costly process, in which the accurate prediction of protein-ligand binding affinity (BAP) represents a critical step. This systematic review article addresses the state of the art in this field, focusing on the evolution and optimization of deep learning (DL) architectures that have emerged as a robust alternative to traditional computational and experimental methods. A taxonomy is proposed that classifies DL models based on their data representation: structure-based models (such as convolutional and graph neural networks), sequence-based models, and hybrid and multimodal approaches. Comparative analysis highlights the superiority of architectures that integrate multimodal information and employ attention mechanisms, demonstrating improved predictive accuracy and generalization capabilities. Nonetheless, there are still issues, such as the limited interpretability of the model or the bias and scarcity of training data. Finally, future research directions emphasize the revolutionary potential of quantum computing, which promises to overcome computational and data limitations, and generative artificial intelligence, which enables de novo molecular design. This work directs the scientific community to the most promising research areas by offering a conceptual framework for comprehending the state of DL-driven BAP today.

Abstract: It is widely recognized that the use of multimode ultrasonic guided waves holds the potential for a comprehensive evaluation of structural damages, including their locations, sizes, and types, in the realm of damage imaging. Recently, a micro-electromechanical system (MEMS) sensor, named 3-D capacitive micromachined ultrasonic transducer (3D-CMUT), has emerged as a promising candidate for detecting 3-D vibrations of multimode ultrasonic guided waves. In this work, 3D-CMUT is introduced for the first time in ultrasonic guided wave-based damage imaging. Initially, for analytical convenience, an idealized design of 3D-CMUT is substituted with a simplified configuration comprising a piston-type cell (sensing z-directional signals) and two orthogonally positioned identical comb-type cells (sensing x- and y-directional signals). Subsequently, finite element method (FEM) simulations are employed to establish models of “a damaged aluminum plate with a circular through-hole” and “an undamaged aluminum plate.” By using the transfer function-based effective method, 3-D scattered signals captured by an array of 3D-CMUTs are rapidly extracted to characterize the circular through-hole. Ultimately, the scattered data are transformed into intuitive damage images using the decomposition of the time-reversal operator and multiple signal classification (DORT-MUSIC) algorithm. The results not only affirm the viability of applying 3D-CMUT in damage imaging but also underscore the advantages of integrating 3-D ultrasonic guided wave information for a more accurate assessment of structural damages. This research may contribute significantly to advancing the applications of 3D-CMUT in nondestructive testing (NDT).

Abstract: Transmission expansion planning aims to establish when and where to install new infrastructure such as transmission lines, cables, generators and transformers in the electrical power system. In this sense, this work aims to propose a methodology to handle demand uncertainty by reducing scenarios through the K-means clustering algorithm, which is used to construct representative demand curves that allow using a static model of stochastic linear optimization with less computational effort, which seeks to minimize the investment and operating costs of the electrical system, meeting the total demand of the system.
